sdd-fleet · a Claude Code plugin · a vision for regulated multi-repo delivery

Deterministic harness.
Probabilistic generation.

sdd-fleet is a spec-driven, multi-agent development system for a regulated, multi-repo estate. Deterministic code — the harness — owns every consequence: which review concerns block a merge, when a gate opens, when work escalates to a person. The model judges only the residue — the judgment calls no rule can settle. A human signs off wherever the blast radius is high. The system is designed for audit and replay rather than for speed.

Harness

Deterministic & fail-closed

Gates, counting, routing, the loop. Reproducible, and not bypassable by an agent.

Model

Probabilistic judgement

Drafting, review, the one contested call. Isolated to a single logged role.

Human

Accountability

Ratification before work spreads to the repos; sign-off on money movement and PII. Accountability the machine cannot supply.

At a glance — the 30-second read

If you read nothing else

For an engineering leader evaluating Claude Code as a way to build from a spec, with auditable output, across a regulated multi-repo estate. This section is the summary; the architecture pages carry the evidence.

What it is

A spec-driven, multi-agent build system where the harness — deterministic code, not the AI model — owns every consequence: which review concerns block a merge, when a gate opens, when work escalates to a human.

What it produces

Per change, a gated, replayable audit trail: every acceptance criterion mapped to a test before code, every contract version-checked, every decision reconstructable from the record.

Where humans stay in control

Contract & epic ratification before any work spreads to the repos, money-movement / PII sign-off, and escalation when review won't converge — enforced by the harness, not by convention.

The tradeoff

Optimised for audit and correctness, not raw speed. Decisive review is front-loaded onto the contract shape up front, rather than spent per-PR after the fact.

▣ What audit reads back

From artifacts that were gated, not asserted, the record supports one sentence per change: “checkout's step-up change was built against catalog snapshot vX, authorise@3.3.0, additive per ADR-217, human sign-off by Y, contract tests green on a write-locked suite.” The rest of this site explains how the harness guarantees that sentence — start with the one rule.

What “replay” means here. Replay is re-deriving the decision trail from the record — which gates ran, what inputs they saw, how the vote counted, who signed — out of .sdd/, the pinned-SHA workspace commit, the registry, and Jira. It is not regenerating identical code: generation is probabilistic, and a re-run may produce a different diff. What is identical on a re-run is every consequence — the gates, the counting, the routing.

Every consequence lives in code; the model judges only the irreducible residue; a human owns the blast radius. Harness Model Human the one rule →

01

What a regulated estate gets

The boundary shows up as four concrete properties. Each one is checkable — by an auditor, a risk officer, or an engineering lead — against the record, not against anyone's account of it.

Replay

Re-derive, don't recollect

Every run reconstructs from the vault record — an auditor re-derives the decision instead of trusting anyone's memory of it.

Hard gates

Sign-off forced by computation

A change touching money-movement or PII data, or reaching N downstream consumers, forces a human gate — by code, not by a reviewer remembering to.

Multi-repo

Contracts hold the estate together

Published contracts are versioned and append-only; pinned consumers are stopped automatically on a breaking bump.

Precision

Auditable and replayable

Every acceptance criterion maps to a test before code; QA reads captured coverage output where tooling exists — never its own opinion of it.

Where humans stay in the loop. Four points require a human signature — enforced by the harness, not left to convention:
epic + contract ratify ◆· money / PII sign-off ◆· blast-radius threshold ◆· escalation on non-convergence ◆
02

One change, end to end

One unit of work — Jira PAY-1843, "add an instant-payout endpoint" — traced through the per-repo machine. Every gate below is enforced by code; none depends on a reviewer remembering to check.

  1. SPEC — the spec and acceptance criteria are authored into the vault .sdd/PAY-1843/. A testability floor refuses any criterion that isn't decidable — no "works correctly", no TBD.
  2. REVIEW — three role lenses fan out and an adversarial pass flags a money-movement concern on the payout path. It survives the survival vote — a concern stands unless refuted with evidence, and the counting is code — so it becomes a blocker the loop must resolve.
  3. FINALIZE ▣ — a fail-closed gate refuses to freeze the spec while review blockers stand. SDD_FLEET_FINALIZE_REFUSE: {"code":2,"reasons":["open-blockers"]} → resolved, then SDD_FLEET_FINALIZE_PASS. No source can be written before this flip.
  4. BUILD — tests first: every criterion is mapped to a failing test (a fail-closed gate refuses source until the mapping is complete), then the test paths are write-locked so the oracle can't be edited to pass; code is generated to turn the locked suite green.
  5. CHANGE_REVIEW — blast radius is computed from the derived service catalog: this endpoint's contract has 4 pinned consumers, and the data carries money_movement.
  6. HANDOFF ◆ — either condition alone forces a human gate, regardless of the machine verdict. The fail-closed gate blocks the handoff until a human signs off, and the approval is pinned to the computed blast-radius signature — it goes stale if the radius widens. SDD_FLEET_HANDOFF_APPROVE_PASS: {"feature":"PAY-1843","signature":"…"}
03

Where to go from here

The site is one linear argument, but three shorter paths through it fit three readers.

Engineering leads

sdd-fleet — a Claude Code plugin · engineered for audit and replay
Deterministic harness · probabilistic generation · human accountability
